title: WoZ Evaluation of ARIA-Live AI Provenance Banners on BLV Screen-Reader Navigation and Credibility Judgment

actors.description: A blind or low-vision adult who uses a screen reader as their primary means of web access, recruited from a disability-led community organization; must have at least 6 months of daily screen-reader use.

A researcher seated in an adjacent room who remotely monitors the participant's screen via screen-share and fires the correct ARIA-live disclosure variant at page-load time by clicking a hotkey on the wizard control panel; the wizard also logs timestamps of all heading-navigation keystrokes observed in the screen-share feed.
A second researcher in the adjacent room who silently codes participant verbal utterances and behavioral hesitations using a predefined coding sheet, without intervening in the session.

A researcher present in the participant's room (or on the same video call for remote sessions) who reads standardized task instructions aloud, manages consent, and administers post-task questionnaires; the facilitator does not explain or comment on any system behavior during tasks.

context.setting: Remote video call (Zoom with screen-share) or in-person in a quiet single-occupancy room at a university accessibility lab; participant uses their own personal device and preferred screen reader in their home or lab environment; wizard and observer join the same Zoom call on a separate device in a different room or off-site.
context.device: Participant's own laptop or desktop running Windows 11 with NVDA 2024.1, or macOS 14 with VoiceOver; browser is Firefox 126 on Windows or Safari 17 on macOS; prototype pages are served from localhost via a Node.js static server tunneled through ngrok so the wizard can inject ARIA-live content remotely; wizard uses a second laptop running Chrome 124 with the wizard control panel HTML page open.
context.session_structure: 0–10 min: facilitator reads consent form and collects signature; participant completes a 5-item screen-reader familiarity screener to confirm eligibility tier. 10–20 min: facilitator gives a warm-up task (navigating a neutral practice article unrelated to the study) to acclimate participant to the think-aloud protocol and confirm screen-share is working for the wizard. 20–55 min: three counterbalanced experimental articles (one per disclosure condition: AI-disclosed-early, AI-disclosed-late, human-framed) presented in a within-subject Latin-square order; each article block is 10–12 min and includes navigation task then credibility rating. 55–70 min: semi-structured debrief interview administered by facilitator using a standardized question script. 70–75 min: facilitator explains the study purpose, answers questions, and provides compensation.

task_flow.participant_action: Participant opens the URL provided by the facilitator in their preferred browser, causing the prototype article page to begin loading; the participant is instructed to 'explore this article as you normally would when deciding whether to read it in full, and think aloud as you go.'
task_flow.system_response: The page loads with a static HTML article (approximately 800 words, structured with H1 title, three H2 section headings, and body paragraphs); the ARIA-live region with the disclosure banner text is present in the DOM but has its content string empty; the wizard's control panel shows which disclosure variant is assigned to this trial and awaits wizard trigger.
wizard_decisions: Confirm from the screen-share that the correct article variant (matching the Latin-square assignment for this participant and trial number) is loaded before firing any control.

Decide whether the participant's screen reader has finished announcing the page title before triggering the disclosure banner injection, so the disclosure does not overlap with the page-load announcement and become unintelligible.
If the page fails to load within 8 seconds, decide whether to instruct the facilitator to reload or to switch to the offline HTML fallback file.

task_flow.participant_action: Within the first 30 seconds of page load, the participant navigates using heading-jump keystrokes (H key in NVDA browse mode, or VO+Command+H in VoiceOver) or arrow keys to survey the article structure; the participant continues to think aloud describing what they are hearing and deciding.
task_flow.system_response: For the AI-disclosed-early condition: the wizard fires the 'inject_early_banner' control immediately after page-load announcement completes, causing the ARIA-live region to announce 'Disclosure: this article was generated by an AI writing assistant and reviewed by a human editor' before the participant reaches the first H2; for the AI-disclosed-late condition: the wizard fires 'inject_late_banner' only after the participant's cursor passes the second H2 heading (detected by watching the screen-share); for the human-framed condition: the wizard fires 'inject_human_banner' immediately after page-load, announcing 'Written by a staff journalist.'
wizard_decisions: Determine from the screen-share and NVDA speech viewer overlay when the participant's virtual cursor has passed the second H2 heading, as the late-banner trigger timing depends on this cursor position rather than elapsed time.

Decide whether the ARIA-live announcement was successfully read aloud by the screen reader (audible in the Zoom audio feed); if it was skipped due to focus being inside a live region, decide whether to re-inject the banner text once.

Judge whether the participant's current navigation mode (browse mode vs. application mode) will correctly expose the ARIA-live region; if the participant has switched to forms mode, flag this in the observation log and hold the banner until they return to browse mode.

task_flow.participant_action: After approximately 8 minutes of free navigation, the facilitator reads a standardized probe aloud: 'Please now find and read the section you consider most important for deciding whether this article is trustworthy, and tell me why you chose it.' The participant navigates to a section and reads it aloud.
task_flow.system_response: No new system event fires during this step; the article content and heading structure remain static; the wizard's control panel displays a read-only timer showing elapsed time since page load and a text field where the wizard types the heading label of whichever H2 the participant lands on, for the navigation log.
wizard_decisions: Log the exact heading text of the section the participant navigates to, and the navigation path taken (sequence of H keystrokes or arrow-key movements audible from screen-share audio), into the timestamped wizard log spreadsheet.

Decide whether any assistive-technology behavior during this step (e.g., NVDA reading a heading twice due to a DOM focus event) constitutes a prototype error that should be flagged for exclusion during analysis, or is attributable to participant navigation style and should be retained.

task_flow.participant_action: The facilitator hands the participant a 7-item credibility rating scale delivered via a second static HTML page (keyboard-navigable radio buttons with visible labels and ARIA labels); the participant completes the scale using their screen reader and submits it.
task_flow.system_response: The credibility rating page loads from localhost and records responses to a local JSON file via a fetch POST to the Node.js server; the wizard's control panel displays a confirmation indicator when the POST succeeds, showing the participant ID, trial number, and condition label so the wizard can verify the correct condition was logged.
wizard_decisions: Verify on the control panel that the condition label recorded in the JSON matches the Latin-square assignment for this participant and trial; if there is a mismatch (e.g., due to URL error), note the discrepancy immediately in the session log so it can be corrected before analysis.

Decide whether to alert the facilitator if the participant appears to be having difficulty operating the radio button form with their screen reader, which would indicate a prototype accessibility failure requiring a verbal workaround (facilitator reads items aloud and records answers on paper).

task_flow.participant_action: After completing all three article trials, the facilitator conducts a 15-minute semi-structured debrief interview using a standardized question script covering: whether the participant noticed any difference between articles, what cues they used to judge credibility, and how (if at all) knowing or not knowing authorship affects their reading behavior.
task_flow.system_response: No prototype system is active during the debrief; the facilitator's printed question script is the only artifact in use; the observer continues coding verbal responses using the coding sheet.
wizard_decisions: Decide, based on the observation log compiled during the three trials, whether to pass a written note to the facilitator through a secure chat channel flagging any specific navigation event (e.g., a participant who re-read the same heading three times after hearing the AI disclosure) that should be probed in the debrief interview to elicit elaboration.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard fires this control immediately after confirming (via screen-share audio) that the screen reader has finished announcing the page H1 title, typically within 3–5 seconds of page load, for any trial assigned to the AI-disclosed-early condition.
wizard_controls.effect: A JavaScript fetch call from the wizard control panel POSTs to a localhost endpoint on the participant's machine (via the ngrok tunnel); the server sets the textContent of the ARIA-live region (role='status', aria-live='polite', aria-atomic='true') to the string 'Disclosure: this article was generated by an AI writing assistant and reviewed by a human editor.' The region is positioned in DOM order immediately after the H1 element so that if the participant is navigating linearly it will be encountered before the first H2.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard fires this control at the moment the participant's screen reader cursor passes the second H2 heading, determined by observing the screen-share video feed and listening for the second H2 heading text in the Zoom audio; this is for trials assigned to the AI-disclosed-late condition.
wizard_controls.effect: Same POST mechanism as inject_early_banner; sets the ARIA-live region textContent to the identical AI disclosure string ('Disclosure: this article was generated by an AI writing assistant and reviewed by a human editor.'); because the region is positioned after the H1 in DOM order, it will not be encountered again by forward heading navigation, so the announcement is delivered only via the polite live-region queue.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard fires this control immediately after confirming the screen reader has finished announcing the H1 title, for any trial assigned to the human-framed condition; timing mirrors inject_early_banner.
wizard_controls.effect: Sets the ARIA-live region textContent to 'Written by a staff journalist.' so that the announcement latency and structural position are identical to the early AI disclosure condition, isolating phrasing as the only variable.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard fires this control if a screen reader bug causes the ARIA-live region to repeat its announcement unexpectedly (audible as a repeated reading in the Zoom audio feed) more than once after the initial injection.
wizard_controls.effect: POSTs to the server endpoint to set the ARIA-live region textContent to an empty string, silencing further announcements from that region for the remainder of the trial.

wizard_controls.triggers: Wizard clicks this control each time they observe the participant pressing the H key or VO+Command+H (audible in the NVDA speech output on the Zoom audio, or visible as a focus-change event in the screen-share), to create a timestamped navigation event record.
wizard_controls.effect: Appends a JSON object {participantId, trialNumber, condition, timestamp, eventType:'heading_jump'} to the session log file on the wizard's local machine; this does not affect the participant-facing prototype in any way.

observable_signals.signal: Heading-navigation keystroke sequence and dwell time per section: the ordered list of H2 headings visited, time elapsed between consecutive heading jumps, and total number of heading jumps before the participant either read body text or declared a section choice.
observable_signals.analysis_plan: For each trial, reconstruct the heading-visit sequence from the wizard's timestamped log. Compute three derived measures per trial: (1) number of heading jumps before first body-text dwell (defined as more than 5 seconds without a heading-jump event), (2) proportion of H2 sections visited before the participant selected one to read in full, and (3) whether the participant reversed direction (navigated backward to a previously visited heading) after hearing the disclosure. Compare these measures across the three conditions (early-AI, late-AI, human) using a within-subject Friedman test across the 8 participants, with individual condition contrasts via Wilcoxon signed-rank. Note any participant whose navigation sequence changed direction within 10 seconds of the ARIA-live announcement as a candidate for qualitative follow-up in the debrief transcript.

observable_signals.signal: Think-aloud verbalization at the moment of ARIA-live banner announcement: the participant's spoken words and non-lexical vocalizations (pauses, repetitions, audible breath) in the 20-second window immediately following the wizard's banner injection.
observable_signals.analysis_plan: From the Zoom audio recording, extract the 20-second clip beginning at the timestamp of each banner injection (retrieved from the wizard log). Two coders independently apply a 4-category scheme to each clip: (A) no verbal acknowledgment of disclosure, (B) verbal acknowledgment without evaluative language (e.g., 'oh, it said something'), (C) verbal acknowledgment with positive or neutral evaluation ('okay, AI-assisted, fine'), (D) verbal acknowledgment with skeptical or adversarial language ('AI? Let me check the sources'). Compute inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) on the 24 clips (8 participants × 3 trials). Tabulate category distribution by condition. Clips coded D are flagged for verbatim quotation in the findings to illustrate the adversarial-check relational mode predicted by the branch hypothesis.

observable_signals.signal: Post-trial credibility rating on 7 items covering perceived accuracy, trustworthiness, author expertise, recency, source transparency, reading intent (would share or cite), and overall quality, each rated 1–7.
observable_signals.analysis_plan: Compute a summed credibility score (range 7–49) per trial per participant. Because the same participant rates all three conditions, compare summed scores across conditions with a within-subject Friedman test. For any item showing a condition difference in median score greater than 1 point (on the 7-point scale), examine the item-level distribution to identify whether the effect is driven by the transparency item specifically (which is theoretically predicted to differ) versus accuracy or expertise items (which would indicate a broader halo effect of the disclosure).

observable_signals.signal: Section-selection behavior: which H2 section the participant navigated to when asked to identify the section most relevant to trustworthiness judgment (Step 3), and the stated reason.
observable_signals.analysis_plan: Wizard log provides the heading label of the selected section for each trial. Code section choices into three pre-defined categories: (1) methodology or sourcing section, (2) conclusion or summary section, (3) introductory or contextual section. Tabulate category by condition to detect whether AI disclosure shifts section-selection toward methodology/sourcing (predicted by adversarial-check hypothesis). Code stated reasons using a 3-category scheme: evidence-based ('it cites sources'), author-based ('I want to know who said this'), or content-based ('it summarizes the main point'). Compute whether author-based reasons increase in frequency in the AI-disclosed conditions relative to human-framed.

observable_signals.signal: Time from page-load completion to first body-text dwell: the elapsed seconds between the wizard's confirmation that the page H1 was announced and the first instance of more than 5 consecutive seconds without a heading-jump event.
observable_signals.analysis_plan: Derive this measure from the wizard event log by subtracting the timestamp of the page-load confirmation event from the timestamp of the first 5-second gap in heading-jump events. Compare medians across the three conditions with a within-subject Friedman test. A longer dwell-onset latency in the AI-disclosed conditions would be consistent with the hypothesis that disclosure triggers additional verification behavior before engaging with body content; a shorter latency would suggest the participant skipped further heading navigation and went directly to reading, which is a competing behavioral pattern worth distinguishing.

failure_cases.scenario: The ARIA-live polite region is not announced by the participant's screen reader because the participant's focus is inside a form element or application-role region at the moment of injection, causing the live-region queue to be suppressed; this is especially likely with NVDA in forms mode, which the participant may enter accidentally if they tab into the credibility form from a previous trial without returning to browse mode.
failure_cases.expected_behavior: The participant continues navigating headings without any verbal acknowledgment of the disclosure, and the think-aloud audio shows no reference to authorship, making the trial indistinguishable from a silent control condition.
failure_cases.wizard_fallback: The wizard monitors the Zoom audio for the banner announcement text within 10 seconds of injection. If no announcement is heard, the wizard sends a text message to the facilitator's phone (visible to the facilitator on a phone placed face-down in their lap, pre-agreed protocol) with the code 'RE-INJECT'. The facilitator says aloud 'Please press Escape to return to browse mode and then re-read from the top of the page.' Once the participant presses Escape, the wizard fires clear_banner followed by the appropriate inject control again. The re-injection is logged as a 'mode-suppression retry' event and the trial data is retained but flagged for sensitivity analysis excluding retry trials.

failure_cases.scenario: The ngrok tunnel between the wizard's control panel and the participant's localhost Node.js server drops during a trial, making all inject controls non-functional; the wizard's POST requests return a network error and the ARIA-live region content is never updated.
failure_cases.expected_behavior: The participant receives no disclosure announcement for that trial regardless of the assigned condition, and the credibility rating form POST also fails, so no data is recorded for that trial.
failure_cases.wizard_fallback: The wizard has a pre-prepared offline fallback: a second laptop running a fully local version of the wizard control panel connected to the participant's machine via a direct LAN cable or local Wi-Fi hotspot (not internet-dependent). If the ngrok tunnel drops, confirmed by two failed POST attempts within 15 seconds, the wizard messages the facilitator with the code 'SWITCH-LOCAL'. The facilitator says 'We need to pause for a moment to fix a technical issue' and the session is paused. The wizard re-establishes the connection using the LAN fallback (estimated 3 minutes). The trial is restarted from the beginning with the same article. If two consecutive tunnel failures occur, the session is ended and the participant is rescheduled; the partial data from completed trials in that session is retained.

failure_cases.scenario: The participant uses a refreshable Braille display as their primary output device and has NVDA speech output muted, so the ARIA-live announcement is delivered to the Braille display rather than as speech; the wizard cannot detect whether the announcement was received by listening to the Zoom audio.
failure_cases.expected_behavior: The wizard's standard confirmation method (listening for the announcement in Zoom audio) fails silently; the wizard cannot distinguish between successful delivery to Braille display and failed injection.
failure_cases.wizard_fallback: During the screener at the start of the session (Step 1), the facilitator asks 'Do you use a Braille display as your primary output?' If yes, the wizard switches to a confirmation protocol: after each injection, the wizard sends the facilitator the code 'CHECK-BRAILLE' and the facilitator asks the participant 'Can you tell me what the last message at the top of the page said?' The participant's response (or absence of response) is used to confirm delivery. This verbal check is scripted as 'just making sure the page loaded correctly for you' to avoid priming the participant about the disclosure. If the participant cannot report the banner text, the wizard re-injects once.

materials_needed: Three prototype article HTML files (article_A_early.html, article_B_late.html, article_C_human.html), each approximately 800 words on a neutral civic topic (e.g., local water infrastructure), structured identically with one H1, three H2 subheadings ('Background', 'Current Status', 'What Experts Say'), and body paragraphs; all three files must pass automated WCAG 2.1 AA checks using axe-core before use.

ARIA-live region injected into each article HTML: a div element with role='status', aria-live='polite', aria-atomic='true', id='provenance-banner', positioned in DOM order immediately after the H1 element, with empty textContent on page load; the element must be present in all three article variants.

Node.js static file server (index.js, approximately 50 lines) that serves the article HTML files and exposes two POST endpoints: /inject-banner (accepts {bannerText: string} and sets the ARIA-live region content via a server-sent event to the page) and /record-rating (accepts {participantId, trial, condition, ratings: []} and appends to ratings.jsonl); server must run on localhost:3000.

Wizard control panel HTML page (wizard_panel.html) with five clearly labeled buttons (INJECT EARLY AI, INJECT LATE AI, INJECT HUMAN, CLEAR BANNER, LOG HEADING NAV), each triggering a fetch POST to the correct endpoint; a trial-assignment display showing the current participant ID, trial number, and assigned condition loaded from a pre-populated Latin-square CSV; and a real-time elapsed-time counter starting when the wizard clicks a 'Page Loaded' confirmation button.

Latin-square condition assignment CSV (latin_square.csv) with 8 rows (one per participant) and 3 columns (trial 1, trial 2, trial 3 condition assignments) covering all 6 permutations of the three conditions across the 8 participants, ensuring counterbalancing.

Credibility rating HTML form (credibility_form.html) with 7 radio-button groups (1–7 scale), each group labeled with visible text and ARIA-label matching the dimension name (Accuracy, Trustworthiness, Author Expertise, Recency, Source Transparency, Intent to Share or Cite, Overall Quality); form submits via fetch POST to /record-rating endpoint.

Wizard observation log spreadsheet template (wizard_log_template.xlsx) with pre-populated columns: Timestamp, ParticipantID, TrialNumber, Condition, EventType (page_load_confirmed, banner_injected, heading_jump, body_dwell_start, mode_suppression_retry, fallback_activated), HeadingLabel (for heading_jump events), Notes.

Observer coding sheet (observer_coding_sheet.pdf, printed) with the 4-category think-aloud coding scheme (A/B/C/D) defined verbatim, a row for each of the 24 trial clips (8 participants × 3 trials), and inter-rater reliability instructions; two printed copies required (one for primary coder, one for reliability coder).

Facilitator script document (facilitator_script.pdf, printed) containing verbatim text for: consent form reading, screener questions with eligibility thresholds, warm-up task instruction, per-trial task instruction ('Explore this article as you normally would…'), Step 3 probe instruction ('Please now find and read the section you consider most important…'), debrief interview questions (8 questions), and debrief explanation of study purpose.

ngrok account with a static subdomain configured to tunnel localhost:3000; ngrok authtoken stored in the wizard's environment; a secondary LAN-based fallback connection plan documented in the session protocol with IP address of the participant's machine pre-confirmed during setup.

Screen-reader software confirmation checklist (one page): before each session, facilitator confirms with participant which screen reader and version they use, which browser, whether they use a Braille display as primary output, and which browse-mode key they use for heading navigation; this information is recorded and shared with the wizard before the session begins.

Zoom license with local recording enabled; participant consent form includes explicit consent for audio and screen recording; recordings stored in a password-protected folder named by participant ID only (no names).

Debrief explanation card (debrief_card.pdf, one page) given to participants after the debrief interview explaining the three conditions, the study hypothesis, and contact information for the research team; written at approximately 8th-grade reading level and available in audio format via a QR code link.
